title: Amendment 1
summary: Add discharging a firearm in an occupied dwelling or other occupied space and any solicitation, attempt, or conspiracy to commit murder to the list of crimes for which a judge may deny bail
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: Expanding judicial authority to deny bail for additional serious crimes (firearm discharge in occupied spaces, murder solicitation/attempt/conspiracy) aligns with conservative criminal justice positions favoring stricter pretrial detention and enhanced public safety measures, opposing the progressive criminal justice reform agenda.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a public safety provision but structurally removes judicial discretion by mandating bail denial eligibility for additional categories of offenses, limiting judges' case-by-case assessment of flight risk and danger.

title: Amendment 2
summary: Prohibit any reduction in a district attorney's compensation during their term in office
econReasoning: This measure protects public employee compensation from reduction, which aligns with conservative fiscal priorities by limiting government spending flexibility and constraining the ability to adjust budgets downward. However, it is not a tax cut or deregulation, so it does not score at the extreme right.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses compensation protections for a law enforcement official and has no direct bearing on social issues such as abortion, gun rights, criminal justice reform, voting rights, or other traditional left-right social divides.

title: Broadcast The Star-Spangled Banner in Public Schools Amendment
summary: Require all public schools to broadcast or arrange for the performance of the first stanza of the Star-Spangled Banner at least once per week
econReasoning: This measure has no meaningful economic dimension—it mandates a procedural requirement for schools without addressing taxation, spending levels, regulation, or market structures.
socialReasoning: Mandatory patriotic observance in public schools aligns with conservative social positions favoring traditional nationalism, religious/civic expression in public institutions, and state-directed cultural messaging rather than individual choice or secular neutrality.

title: Require Special Election for Lieutenant Governor Vacancies Amendment
summary: Require the office of lieutenant governor to be filled by election if a vacancy occurs more than 60 days before the next general election
econReasoning: This measure addresses the procedural mechanism for filling a vacant lieutenant governor position and has no direct economic impact, taxation, spending, or market implications.
socialReasoning: This measure concerns electoral procedure for filling an executive vacancy and does not implicate social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting rights, or other socially divisive domains.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a routine election requirement but structurally shifts power from gubernatorial appointment authority to the electorate, a non-obvious institutional effect that merits disclosure.

title: Require the Pledge of Allegiance and Allow Student-Led Prayer in Public Schools Amendment
summary: Require public schools to conduct the pledge of allegiance each day, and allow student-led prayer to be conducted
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it addresses curricular and religious practice requirements in public schools rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: The measure combines two traditionally conservative social positions: mandatory patriotic observance (Pledge of Allegiance) and religious expression in schools (student-led prayer). Both align strongly with right-wing social priorities that emphasize traditional values, religious accommodation, and nationalist symbolism.

title: Citizenship Voting Requirement Initiative
summary: Provide that "Only a person who is a citizen" may vote
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation, or market policy.
socialReasoning: Citizenship voting requirements align with conservative immigration restriction positions and have been championed predominantly by Republican candidates and organizations. This measure restricts the electorate based on immigration status, a signature conservative social policy position.

title: Establish Campaign Contribution Limits for State and Local Elections Initiative
summary: Establish new campaign contribution limits for campaigns for state and local office
econReasoning: Campaign contribution limits are a form of financial regulation typically supported by Democrats and opposed by Republicans. The measure restricts the flow of money in politics, which aligns with left-leaning preferences for regulated markets and reduced private influence over public processes.
socialReasoning: Campaign contribution limits have no direct connection to social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or other traditional left-right social policy divides.
framingNote: The measure is framed neutrally as establishing 'limits,' but campaign finance restrictions structurally shift political power by affecting which candidates and groups can compete effectively, which is a material institutional effect that extends beyond a transparent procedural change.

title: Repeal Top-Four Ranked-Choice Voting Initiative
summary: Repeal the top-four ranked-choice voting (RCV) system that was adopted in 2020
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it concerns electoral mechanics rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Repealing ranked-choice voting aligns with conservative/Republican positions. Republicans have predominantly opposed RCV adoption, viewing it as advantageous to Democrats and third parties, while supporting traditional plurality voting systems that favor the two-party structure.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a simple 'repeal' but structurally restores the previous plurality voting system, which has different strategic effects on candidate viability, coalition-building, and party competition—a material procedural change that may not be obvious to voters unfamiliar with voting system mechanics.

title: Designate Drug Cartels as Terrorist Organizations and Require State DHS to Address Threats Measure
summary: Declare drug cartels to be terrorist organizations
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it designates drug cartels as terrorist organizations and directs state DHS to address threats, which involves administrative/law enforcement classification rather than tax, spending, or market policy.
socialReasoning: The measure aligns strongly with conservative/right-wing positions by expanding anti-drug enforcement authority, supporting law-and-order approaches to drug trafficking, and framing immigration-adjacent threats in security terms—all consistent with Republican-backed hardline drug and border security policies.
framingNote: Designating cartels as 'terrorist organizations' emotionally anchors the measure to terrorism prevention but structurally expands government enforcement authority and DHS powers well beyond traditional counterterrorism into drug trafficking enforcement.

title: Limit Local Tax Rate on Groceries to 2% of Base Price and Require Voter Approval to Increase Tax Rate Measure
summary: Prohibit local government from imposing or increasing a tax on the sale of food items without voter approval and cap the tax rate at 2%
econReasoning: This measure restricts local government's taxing authority and caps tax rates, which aligns with right-leaning preferences for tax limitations and reduced government revenue collection. The requirement for voter approval on tax increases reflects conservative skepticism of unilateral government taxation.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses taxation of food items and has no direct connection to social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting rights, or other culture war dimensions.
framingNote: The title frames this as 'relief' for groceries, but the structural effect is a revenue restriction on local governments that shifts fiscal burdens elsewhere or reduces public services—a material distinction not obvious from the consumer-benefit framing.

title: Prohibit Taxes or Fees on Miles Traveled in Motor Vehicle Amendment
summary: Prohibit the state and local governments from imposing taxes or fees based on vehicle miles traveled and from enacting rules to monitor or limit vehicle miles traveled without the person’s consent
econReasoning: This measure opposes a potential tax mechanism (vehicle miles traveled tax), aligning with tax-cut and anti-regulation positions typically favored by the political right. It restricts government revenue-raising authority and reflects skepticism toward new taxation schemes.
socialReasoning: The measure has no significant social dimension—it does not address abortion, voting rights, gun rights, LGBTQ+ issues, criminal justice, drug policy, or other traditional social axis dividers.
framingNote: The measure frames opposition to a mileage tax as prohibiting 'taxes or fees' and 'monitoring' without consent, using rights language (privacy/freedom from surveillance) to anchor what is fundamentally a tax restriction proposal that may obscure its primary fiscal intent.

title: Citizenship Requirement for Voting Amendment
summary: Provide that only U.S. citizens may vote in state or local elections
econReasoning: This measure addresses voting eligibility requirements and has no direct economic dimension regarding taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms.
socialReasoning: Citizenship requirements for voting are a signature conservative position on immigration and electoral access, strongly opposed by progressive advocates of voting rights expansion. This aligns with the Republican/right side of the social axis.

title: Natural Resources Commission Bond Measure
summary: Authorize the Arkansas Natural Resources Commission to issue up to $500 million in general obligation bonds to fund water-related infrastructure projects including water treatment and transportation, waste disposal, pollution abatement, drainage, irrigation, flood control, and wetlands and aquatic resources infrastructure
econReasoning: This measure authorizes $500 million in public bonds for water infrastructure, representing significant government investment in public goods and environmental protection—priorities typically aligned with Democratic/left economic positions. The measure relies on public financing and regulation of water resources rather than market-driven or privatized solutions.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses water infrastructure and environmental management with no direct bearing on social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or other culture-war dimensions of the social axis.

title: Right to Keep and Bear Arms Amendment
summary: Provide that the right to keep and bear arms is "a natural, fundamental, and individual right that shall not be infringed"
econReasoning: This measure addresses constitutional gun rights and has no direct economic dimension regarding taxation, regulation, or market policy.
socialReasoning: Gun rights amendments are a core conservative position in American politics, consistently aligned with the right side of the social axis. This amendment's strong language protecting the right to keep and bear arms directly reflects Republican/conservative policy priorities.

title: Allow Public Financing of Election Campaigns Measure
summary: Allow the state and local governments to create programs that provide candidates with public funds under spending limits and eligibility rules
econReasoning: Public financing of elections requires government funding and spending, which aligns with progressive positions on public programs and wealth redistribution. This represents a left-leaning approach to campaign finance rather than market-driven or deregulatory positioning.
socialReasoning: Public campaign financing is championed by Democrats and progressives as a voting rights and democratic participation expansion measure intended to reduce wealthy donor influence. Republicans typically oppose it as excessive government intervention, making this a left-aligned position on democratic access.
framingNote: The measure frames itself as enabling 'public financing' but structurally alters the balance of political power by reducing dependence on private donors and wealthy individuals, which shifts influence away from monied interests—a material structural effect that extends beyond simple fiscal mechanics.

title: Eliminate Successor Election at a State Officer Recall Election Amendment
summary: Eliminate the successor election when a state officer is recalled, thereby leaving the office vacant until it is filled according to state law
econReasoning: This measure addresses the procedural mechanics of recall elections and does not substantively alter taxation, spending, regulation, or economic distribution. The fiscal impact is negligible and orthogonal to economic ideology.
socialReasoning: The measure concerns only the administrative process for filling vacated state offices following a recall and has no bearing on social issues such as abortion, voting rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or LGBTQ+ protections.
framingNote: The title frames this as a technical 'elimination' of a successor election, but the structural effect materially shifts power by removing voters' ability to select a replacement and instead deferring to state law succession procedures, which typically vest appointment authority in remaining executive officials rather than the electorate.

title: Second Mortgage Homebuyer Program and Revenue Bond Initiative
summary: Establish a homeownership loan program for households with incomes that do not exceed 200% of the area median income for a family of the same size in the county that requires at least a 3% of the purchase price of the qualified home for the downpayment
econReasoning: This measure establishes a government-backed homeownership program targeting lower-to-middle income households, representing progressive fiscal intervention through a public loan program funded by revenue bonds. The program redistributes public resources to expand homeownership access for income-qualified families, aligning with left-leaning economic policy approaches.
socialReasoning: The measure contains no provisions related to social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, drug policy, or religious exemptions. It is purely economic in character.

title: Two-Thirds Vote Requirement for Special Taxes and Charter City Real Estate Transfer Tax Prohibition Initiative
summary: Require a two-thirds vote by the electorate to enact special local taxes enacted by successful citizen initiative campaigns
econReasoning: This measure raises the threshold for enacting special taxes via citizen initiative from a simple majority to two-thirds, making tax increases more difficult to implement. This aligns with right-leaning fiscal conservatism favoring lower taxes and reduced government revenue.
socialReasoning: The measure has no substantive social policy dimension; it is purely procedural regarding tax approval mechanisms and contains no provisions affecting civil rights, identity, or social issues.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a voting requirement rule but structurally shifts power by making it substantially harder for citizens to enact tax policy through direct democracy, effectively privileging the status quo and constraining the initiative process itself.

title: Vote Requirements for Initiatives Requiring Supermajority Votes Amendment
summary: Require initiatives that change vote thresholds to supermajority votes to pass by the same vote requirement as is being proposed
econReasoning: This measure raises the procedural bar for ballot initiatives that propose supermajority voting requirements, making it harder to pass fiscal measures that would restrict taxation or spending through supermajority thresholds. This aligns with conservative/right preferences for limiting the ease of passing restrictive fiscal measures and protecting existing tax authority.
socialReasoning: This measure is purely procedural and structural, addressing ballot mechanics rather than substantive social policy questions.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a technical requirement about vote thresholds, but it structurally shifts power by making it harder for ballot initiatives to impose supermajority voting requirements on future fiscal measures, effectively protecting the legislature's fiscal authority.

title: Notification of U.S. Department of Homeland Security for Certain Criminal Charges and Immigration Status Initiative
summary: Require law enforcement to notify the U.S. Department of Homeland Security when a person is charged with a crime of violence or has a prior felony conviction and the person’s immigration status cannot be determined
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension, as it concerns law enforcement procedures and information-sharing rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: This measure aligns strongly with conservative immigration restriction positions by mandating law enforcement notification to federal immigration authorities for individuals charged with crimes of violence or with prior felony convictions. It reflects the right's emphasis on immigration enforcement and public safety through stricter procedural requirements.
framingNote: The measure anchors on 'crimes of violence' but structurally mandates notification for any person whose immigration status 'cannot be determined'—a broader trigger that extends beyond the named category and expands DHS notification authority based on an administrative status check.

title: Penalties for Fentanyl Sale and Possession Initiative
summary: Increase penalties for fentanyl-related felonies, require treatment for certain drug offenses, and limit sentencing exceptions in fentanyl-related crimes that cause another person’s death
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension—it does not address taxation, regulation of markets, public spending, or economic distribution mechanisms.
socialReasoning: Increased penalties for drug offenses, mandatory minimums for fentanyl crimes, and restrictions on sentencing exceptions align with conservative criminal justice positions opposing drug legalization and favoring stronger law enforcement—a right-leaning stance on the social axis.
framingNote: The measure frames fentanyl deaths as an urgent public health crisis requiring strict penalties, but its structural effect is to remove judicial discretion through mandatory minimums and sentencing restrictions, a classic criminal justice hardline position.

title: Prohibit Surgeries for Treatment in Response to Minors' Perception of Sex or Gender Measure
summary: Prohibit healthcare professionals from providing surgeries to persons under age 18 for the purpose of “treatment in response to a minor’s perception of sex or gender”
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it does not address taxation, redistribution, regulation of markets, or public spending.
socialReasoning: This measure restricts gender-affirming medical procedures for minors, which aligns with conservative social positions on gender and transgender policy. Such restrictions are predominantly backed by Republican/right-leaning voters and opposed by Democratic/left-leaning voters who prioritize LGBTQ+ protections.

title: Require Life in Prison for Human Trafficking of Minors Measure
summary: Require life in prison without parole or release for persons convicted of human trafficking a child for sexual servitude
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms. It is purely a criminal justice policy.
socialReasoning: Mandatory minimum sentencing without judicial discretion is a hallmark conservative/right-wing criminal justice position. Republicans have historically championed mandatory minimums and severe penalties, while progressives have advocated for judicial discretion and criminal justice reform. This measure aligns strongly with the conservative approach to sentencing.
framingNote: The measure's appeal to child protection and victim safety obscures its core structural effect: the elimination of judicial discretion in sentencing by imposing an inflexible life-without-parole mandate.

title: Sex Requirement for School and College Sports Initiative
summary: Require all K-12 and collegiate school sports teams to be separated based on biological sex, unless the team is co-ed
econReasoning: This measure has no economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: This measure aligns strongly with conservative social policy by restricting transgender participation in school sports and reinforcing traditional biological sex-based categories in athletics, a position predominantly backed by Republican voters and opposed by progressive advocates for LGBTQ+ inclusion.

title: Changes to Budget Stabilization Fund Amendment
summary: Increase the amount of funds that may be retained in the budget stabilization fund from 10% to 25% of net general revenue among other changes
econReasoning: This measure increases the budget stabilization (rainy day) fund cap from 10% to 25% of net general revenue, reflecting a conservative preference for fiscal restraint, reduced spending flexibility, and accumulation of reserves rather than immediate government expenditure. Republicans typically support larger emergency reserves and constrained budgets, while Democrats prefer greater spending capacity for public programs.
socialReasoning: This is a purely fiscal measure concerning budget mechanics with no direct bearing on social issues such as abortion, gun rights, criminal justice, voting rights, or other social policy dimensions.

title: Exempt Tangible Personal Property Used for Agriculture or Agritourism from Property Taxes Amendment
summary: Exempt agricultural-related tangible personal property, such as farm equipment or tools, from property taxes
econReasoning: This measure reduces tax revenue by exempting agricultural tangible personal property from property taxes, representing a tax cut favoring a specific economic sector. This aligns with conservative/right-leaning fiscal policy that prioritizes reduced taxation and supports business and agricultural interests.
socialReasoning: The measure has no significant social dimension—it addresses only tax treatment of agricultural equipment and does not engage with social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting access, or other cultural divides.
framingNote: The measure frames the benefit as a straightforward exemption for farmers, but obscures the fiscal mechanism—specifically, which local governments bear the revenue loss and whether the exemption's cost is ultimately shifted to other property taxpayers or public services.

title: Authorize Creation of Next Generation 9-1-1 Fund Amendment
summary: Allow the state legislature to create and dedicate revenue to a fund titled the Georgia Next Generation 9-1-1 Fund
econReasoning: This measure authorizes dedicated revenue for 911 infrastructure, representing a modest public investment in emergency services. While not a tax cut or deregulation, it is a straightforward infrastructure funding mechanism without significant redistribution or regulatory expansion.
socialReasoning: This measure has no material social policy dimension—it addresses emergency services infrastructure and does not implicate abortion, voting rights, criminal justice, gun rights, LGBTQ+ protections, or other social wedge issues.

title: Increase Acre Limit for Agriculture and Timber Conservation Use Property Tax Classification Amendment
summary: Increase the maximum acreage of agricultural and timber property that can be classified as bona fide conservation use property, which is taxed at 40% of the land's value, from 2,000 acres to 4,000 acres
econReasoning: This measure reduces tax liability for agricultural and timber property owners by doubling the acreage eligible for preferential tax treatment (40% of land value), representing a targeted tax cut favoring property owners and rural/agricultural interests. The expansion of a tax preference aligns with conservative economic priorities of reducing tax burden on specific constituencies.
socialReasoning: The measure has no bearing on social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting access, drug policy, religious exemptions, or other social conservative/progressive dividing lines.
framingNote: The title emphasizes 'conservation use' as a noble environmental/preservation goal, but the structural effect is a tax reduction for qualifying landowners; the conservation framing obscures that this is primarily a fiscal benefit to agricultural and timber interests rather than a conservation mandate.

title: Require Nonpartisan Elections for Probate Judges Amendment
summary: Require that probate judges be elected on a nonpartisan basis
econReasoning: This measure concerns the electoral structure for probate judges and has no direct fiscal, tax, or economic redistribution implications.
socialReasoning: Nonpartisan elections are a procedural reform with no direct bearing on social issues like abortion, gun rights, criminal justice, or LGBTQ+ rights.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a neutral procedural reform (nonpartisan elections), but it structurally shifts how judicial power is allocated and potentially influences which candidates succeed by removing party label cues that voters use to predict judicial behavior on substantive issues.

title: Increase Time for Senate to Act on Judicial Appointments Amendment
summary: Increase the timeframe for the Senate to consider and act on appointments of judges and justices from 30 days to 60 days for appointments made between April 1 and December 31 when the Senate is not in regular session or is soon to adjourn the regular session
econReasoning: This measure concerns judicial appointment procedures and has no direct economic dimension regarding taxation, spending, regulation, or market policy.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses the timeline for Senate consideration of judicial appointments and does not substantively implicate social policy issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting rights, or religious exemptions.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a neutral procedural amendment about timeframes, but structurally shifts power by extending the Senate's consideration period, potentially affecting the Governor's ability to staff the judiciary and altering the political dynamics around judicial appointments during specific legislative windows.

title: HJR 4
summary: Provide that only the state legislature has the authority to legalize marijuana, narcotics, or other psychoactive substances
econReasoning: This measure does not substantively address taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution. It is purely a matter of legislative authority over drug policy and has no direct economic dimension.
socialReasoning: This measure restricts the pathway to drug legalization by centralizing authority in the state legislature and blocking ballot initiatives or other mechanisms for legalization. Anti-drug-legalization positions align with conservative/right social values, and this represents a clear restriction on drug policy liberalization.
framingNote: The measure is framed as protecting legislative authority over drug legalization, but its structural effect is to prevent ballot initiatives and popular referenda from legalizing drugs—a significant shift in the mechanism of democratic decision-making on this issue.

title: HJR 6
summary: Add constitutional language designating English as the official language of Idaho
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: Official English language designations are characteristically supported by Republican and conservative voters as a cultural/identity policy and are opposed by Democrats and progressives who view such measures as restrictive toward immigrant and multilingual communities. This aligns with the conservative social axis.

title: Official State Gun Question
summary: Ask voters to choose the official state gun of Idaho
econReasoning: This measure has no economic dimension; it is purely symbolic and involves no taxation, spending, regulation, or market effects.
socialReasoning: Designating an official state gun is a symbolic affirmation of gun rights and gun culture, positions strongly associated with conservative social values. This type of measure is typically championed by Republican and gun-rights advocates.

title: Public Question 1
summary: Provide for the denial of bail when the accused is found to pose a substantial risk to another person
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it addresses criminal procedure rather than taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms.
socialReasoning: Denying bail based on risk assessment aligns with conservative criminal justice positions emphasizing public safety, victim protection, and judicial authority to restrict defendant liberty—positions traditionally championed by Republican-aligned voters and law-and-order platforms.
framingNote: The measure frames bail denial as a public safety protection ('substantial risk to another person') while structurally removing judicial discretion and expanding pretrial detention authority, which are hallmarks of mandatory minimum-style policies that constrain judicial flexibility.

title: Residency Requirements for City and Town Court Judges Amendment
summary: Require city and town court judges to reside in either the county where the court is located or the bordering county closest to the court
econReasoning: This measure addresses judicial residency requirements and has no direct economic impact on taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms.
socialReasoning: The measure concerns geographic eligibility for judicial office and does not implicate social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice reform, voting rights, or religious exemptions.

title: Elections for Supreme Court Justices Amendment
summary: Make the Kansas Supreme Court an elected office and to abolish the court's nominating commission
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it concerns the selection mechanism for judges rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Electing judges and abolishing a nominating commission aligns with conservative priorities of democratic accountability and reducing institutional/expert gatekeeping. Republicans have broadly favored judicial elections, while Democrats have opposed them as threats to judicial independence; this measure reflects the conservative position on judicial selection.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a straightforward procedural reform (making the court 'elected'), but it structurally shifts power from a merit-based nominating commission to direct popular election—a consequential change in how judicial independence and accountability are balanced.

title: Citizenship Voting Requirement Amendment
summary: Amend the state constitution to state that only a citizen of the United States is eligible to vote
econReasoning: This measure has no meaningful economic dimension—it addresses voting eligibility criteria rather than taxation, spending, regulation, or market policy.
socialReasoning: Citizenship voting requirements are a core conservative/right-aligned position on immigration and voter access restrictions. Republicans predominantly support such measures while Democrats typically oppose them as potentially exclusionary.

title: Limit Governor's Pardon Power and Commutations Near General Election Amendment
summary: Prohibit the governor from issuing a pardon or commuting a sentence 60 days prior to the gubernatorial election and until after the fifth Tuesday succeeding the election
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms. It is purely a procedural governance matter.
socialReasoning: This measure restricts executive clemency power near elections, reflecting conservative concerns about unchecked executive authority and skepticism toward broad pardon discretion. Restrictions on pardon/commutation powers align with right-leaning emphasis on crime control and limiting judicial/executive leniency.
framingNote: The measure frames as election-integrity reform, but its structural effect is to constrain gubernatorial executive power during a politically sensitive window, shifting discretionary authority away from the executive branch during election periods.

title: Amendment 1
summary: Allow the legislature to add officers, positions, and employees to the unclassified civil service
econReasoning: This measure allows the legislature to expand the unclassified civil service by adding officers, positions, and employees, which increases government workforce flexibility and reduces procedural constraints—a deregulatory approach favored by conservatives seeking to reduce civil service protections and union influence. The ability to expand unclassified positions (typically exempt from civil service rules) without restrictions aligns with right-leaning preferences for executive/legislative control over government operations.
socialReasoning: This measure has no material social dimension; it concerns only the structure of civil service classification and does not address any rights, cultural issues, or social policy matters.
framingNote: The title frames this as a neutral administrative adjustment, but it materially shifts power from civil service protections to legislative discretion over government hiring and employment conditions.

title: Amendment 2
summary: Add the St. George Community School System to the list of school systems that are regarded and treated as parishes for purposes of operating a school system
econReasoning: This measure adds a specific school system to a list of systems treated as parishes for operational purposes, a technical governance classification with no significant implications for taxation, redistribution, regulation, or market-driven policy.
socialReasoning: The measure is a procedural/administrative clarification regarding school district governance structure with no direct bearing on abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting rights, drug policy, religious exemptions, or traditional values.

title: Amendment 3
summary: Repeal the three education revenue funds and apply that money to the Teachers' Retirement System
econReasoning: This measure redirects existing education revenue funds toward the Teachers' Retirement System, which is a fiscal restructuring that aligns with conservative priorities of controlling public spending and addressing pension liabilities without raising new revenue. The reallocation reflects a right-leaning approach to managing government expenditures and benefits.
socialReasoning: This measure has no significant social dimension; it is purely a fiscal and budgetary matter regarding education funding and retirement benefits.

title: Amendment 4
summary: Allow parishes to exempt business inventory from property taxes
econReasoning: This measure allows parishes to exempt business inventory from property taxes, which is a tax cut favoring business interests and aligns with right-leaning fiscal policy priorities of reducing tax burdens on commerce and private enterprise.
socialReasoning: This measure has no social dimension—it addresses only tax policy on business inventory and does not touch social issues like abortion, gun rights, LGBTQ+ protections, criminal justice, or other socially divisive topics.

title: Amendment 5
summary: Increase the judicial retirement age from 70 to 75
econReasoning: This measure adjusts the mandatory retirement age for judges and has no direct impact on taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms. The fiscal implications (if any) are negligible and administratively neutral.
socialReasoning: Judicial retirement age rules do not implicate abortion rights, LGBTQ+ protections, criminal justice sentencing philosophy, voting rights, gun rights, drug policy, or other core social issues. This is a personnel/administrative matter without ideological content.

title: Raise Income Limit to $150,000 for Special Property Tax Assessment Amendment
summary: Raise the income limit to qualify for the property tax special assessment level from $100,000 to $150,000
econReasoning: Raising the income limit for a special property tax assessment is a tax benefit targeted at higher-income households, reducing their tax burden and aligning with conservative fiscal priorities of lower taxation. This represents a tax cut for a specific income bracket, consistent with right-leaning economic policy.
socialReasoning: This measure has no social policy dimension; it is purely a fiscal/tax policy matter with no implications for social issues.
framingNote: The measure frames the change as a benefit to households earning $100,000-$150,000, but obscures the fiscal cost to the state and which taxpayers ultimately bear that burden through reduced state revenue or other compensatory measures.

title: Birth Certificate Sex Requirement for Public School Sports Initiative
summary: Require that public school sports teams designated for girls or boys be limited to students of the corresponding sex, as recorded on their birth certificate
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension. It addresses school sports eligibility rules without affecting taxation, regulation of markets, or public spending priorities.
socialReasoning: This measure restricts transgender and non-binary students' participation in sports aligned with their gender identity, requiring alignment with biological sex at birth. This aligns strongly with conservative social positions on traditional definitions of sex and gender, similar to other anti-trans policies that score on the right side of the social axis.

title: Changes to Collective Bargaining for State Employees Amendment
summary: Require the budget submitted by the governor to provide for expenditures for wages, hours, benefits, and other terms and conditions of employment provided by a memorandum of understanding that covers state employees and provides for statutory changes to collective bargaining for state employees, including requiring the selection of a neutral arbitrator and establishing a process of arbitration
econReasoning: This measure strengthens collective bargaining rights for state employees and mandates budget provisions for union-negotiated wages and benefits, which aligns with progressive labor policies and public sector worker protections. The arbitration requirement and statutory protections for collective bargaining reflect left-leaning support for unionization and worker power against management.
socialReasoning: This measure has no material social dimension; it addresses labor relations and fiscal procedures rather than social values, identity issues, or cultural conflicts.

title: Commission on Judicial Disabilities Vacancies Amendment
summary: Allow the chair of the Commission on Judicial Disabilities to appoint former members to temporarily fill vacancies or allow the governor to appoint a substitute member or extend the term of an existing member
econReasoning: This measure involves procedural appointments to a judicial ethics commission and has no direct fiscal, tax, or economic policy implications.
socialReasoning: The measure addresses administrative vacancy-filling procedures for a judicial disciplinary body and contains no substantive social policy content related to rights, freedoms, or social issues.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a neutral administrative fix for filling vacancies, but it structurally shifts appointment power from the governor to the commission chair, potentially affecting judicial oversight independence.

title: Firearm Regulations Referendum
summary: Uphold the legislation, An Act Modernizing Firearm Laws (H. 4885), passed in 2024
econReasoning: The measure includes minor economic regulatory components through enhanced licensing and reporting requirements, but these are not primary drivers of the policy. The economic dimension is modest and tilts slightly left toward regulation.
socialReasoning: This measure restricts firearm access through modernized regulations including enhanced background checks, licensing requirements, and waiting periods—positions strongly aligned with Democratic/progressive social policy. Gun regulation scores negatively on the social axis (opposite of gun rights expansion).

title: Constitutional Convention Question
summary: Asks voters whether to hold a state constitutional convention
econReasoning: A constitutional convention question is a procedural/institutional measure that does not inherently favor redistribution or tax policy on either side; the economic implications depend entirely on what a hypothetical convention might propose.
socialReasoning: This measure is procedural and does not directly address social policy dimensions such as abortion, gun rights, voting access, or criminal justice; any social implications would depend on convention outcomes.

title: Amendment 1
summary: Renew the existing sales and use tax of 0.1% for 10 years to fund state parks and soil and water conservation
econReasoning: This measure renews an existing sales tax to fund public programs (state parks and soil/water conservation), which aligns with left-leaning support for public funding and environmental stewardship. However, it is a renewal of an existing tax rather than a new progressive initiative, and sales taxes are regressive, moderating the leftward lean.
socialReasoning: The measure has no significant social dimension—it addresses environmental and recreational conservation with no bearing on social issues like abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, or criminal justice.

title: Amendment 2
summary: Require the Jackson County Assessor to be an elected position
econReasoning: Requiring an elected assessor reflects distrust of appointed bureaucracy and preference for direct democratic accountability over technocratic administration, a position more aligned with conservative skepticism of government institutions. However, the measure is fundamentally about governance structure rather than substantive tax or fiscal policy, limiting its economic ideology score.
socialReasoning: This measure concerns the selection method for a county administrative position and has no bearing on social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting rights, or other cultural divides.

title: Amendment 3
summary: Prohibit abortion in most cases, and prohibit gender transition procedures for minors under eighteen
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it does not address taxation, regulation, public spending, or market structure.
socialReasoning: The measure restricts both abortion access and gender transition procedures for minors, positions strongly aligned with conservative social policy priorities that oppose abortion rights and anti-trans policies.

title: Amendment 4
summary: Require the majority of voters from each congressional district to approve initiative petitions to amend the constitution, prohibit foreign nationals from contributing to ballot measure campaigns, among other provisions
econReasoning: The measure addresses ballot initiative procedures and campaign finance restrictions, which have no direct economic redistribution or tax implications. These are procedural reforms rather than fiscal policy measures.
socialReasoning: The measure includes two conservative-aligned provisions: raising the threshold for constitutional amendments (making direct democracy harder, favored by conservatives) and prohibiting foreign nationals from campaign contributions (election security/immigration-related, favored by conservatives). These align with right-leaning priorities around limiting ballot initiatives and immigration restrictions.
framingNote: The measure is framed as election 'reform' but structurally shifts power away from statewide voters toward congressional districts and incumbent legislative bodies by raising barriers to constitutional amendments via popular initiative.

title: Income Tax Elimination and Sales Tax Changes Amendment
summary: Phase out individual income tax based on revenue growth and make other tax-related changes
econReasoning: This measure phases out individual income tax and adjusts sales tax structures, representing a significant tax cut and shift away from progressive income taxation toward consumption-based taxation. These are core conservative fiscal positions aligned with Republican tax policy preferences.
socialReasoning: This amendment addresses only tax policy and revenue structures with no provisions affecting social issues, abortion, gun rights, criminal justice, voting access, LGBTQ+ protections, or other socially-divisive policy domains.
framingNote: The title emphasizes income tax elimination while downplaying that the mechanism relies on contingent revenue growth triggers and includes sales tax changes that may increase effective tax burden on lower-income households, obscuring the actual distributional impact of the tax restructuring.

title: Change State Legislative Term Limits from Two to Three Consecutive Terms Amendment
summary: Change the legislative term limit from two consecutive four-year terms to three
econReasoning: Term limit extension has no direct bearing on taxation, spending, regulation, or market policy. The measure is procedurally structural rather than economically substantive.
socialReasoning: Term limits do not implicate social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, voting access, or religious expression. The measure is neutral on social dimensions.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a simple term limit adjustment, but it structurally shifts power toward legislative incumbency and institutional continuity, potentially reducing electoral turnover and voter influence over legislative composition.

title: Question 6
summary: Provide for a state constitutional right to abortion before fetal viability
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it establishes a constitutional right but does not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: The measure protects abortion rights before fetal viability, which is a core progressive/Democrat-aligned position on the social axis. This aligns with the left's emphasis on reproductive autonomy and bodily rights, placing it firmly in the progressive category similar to same-sex marriage protections.

title: Question 7
summary: Require voters to present photo identification when voting in person or to provide the last four digits of their driver’s license or Social Security number when voting by mail
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it imposes procedural requirements for voting but does not address taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or wealth redistribution.
socialReasoning: Voter ID requirements are a signature conservative/Republican position on the social axis, aligned with restricting voting access and addressing election integrity concerns as framed by the right. This measure scores strongly positive on the social axis reflecting Republican-backed voting restriction policies.

title: Eliminate Office of Register of Probate Amendment
summary: Eliminate the office of the register of probate
econReasoning: Eliminating a state office reduces government bureaucracy and overhead, which aligns with conservative/Republican preference for smaller government and fiscal efficiency. This represents a cost-cutting measure that typically appeals to those favoring leaner public administration.
socialReasoning: The measure has no significant social dimension—it is purely a structural/administrative matter concerning court probate procedures and does not implicate social issues like abortion, gun rights, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, or voting.

title: Board of Regents Nominating Committees Amendment
summary: Make changes to the Board of Regents, including establishing nominating committees to provide lists of candidates, provide that student members are appointed from a list provided by student governing body, and prohibit non-student members from changing political party 12 months preceding appointment
econReasoning: This measure addresses governance structure and administrative procedures for a public university board with no direct fiscal, tax, or economic policy implications.
socialReasoning: The measure includes a restriction on non-student board members changing political party within 12 months of appointment, which functions as a conservative-aligned constraint on political switching. However, the nominating committee structure and student representation provisions lack clear ideological alignment. The party-switching restriction tilts the measure slightly rightward as a structural limitation on political behavior.
framingNote: The measure is framed as improving Board of Regents governance through nominating committees, but it structurally shifts appointment power and constraints by introducing nominating committees as gatekeepers and adding a political party restriction that limits board member autonomy.

title: Eliminate Governor’s Pocket Veto and Require Veto Explanations Amendment
summary: Require bills to become law if the governor does not sign or veto them within the required timeframe, eliminating the governor's pocket veto
econReasoning: This measure addresses gubernatorial veto procedures and does not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources. It has no economic dimension.
socialReasoning: This measure concerns the mechanics of legislative procedure and executive authority. It does not address social issues such as rights, values, criminal justice, or cultural policy.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a neutral procedural reform ('eliminate pocket veto'), but it structurally shifts power from the executive branch to the legislature by converting inaction into automatic law passage rather than bill failure.

title: Establish Salary for State Legislators Amendment
summary: Establish a salary for state legislators equal to the annual median household income of the state
econReasoning: Establishing legislator salaries at median household income level increases public sector compensation and represents government investment in democratic institutions, aligning with progressive priorities for adequate public service funding. This contrasts with conservative preferences for limiting government spending and legislator compensation.
socialReasoning: This measure has no significant social dimension; it addresses legislative compensation structure rather than social policy issues like rights, identity, or criminal justice.

title: Higher Education Improvements Bond Issue
summary: Authorize up to $230 million in general obligation bonds for capital improvements and acquisitions at higher education institutions, special schools, and tribal schools, funded by a statewide property tax
econReasoning: This measure authorizes $230 million in public bonds for higher education infrastructure, representing significant public investment in education funded through progressive property taxation. Bond-financed public education spending and tax-funded capital improvements align with Democratic/left-leaning economic priorities.
socialReasoning: The measure addresses educational infrastructure without touching social issues like abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or religious exemptions. No social axis dimension is present.

title: Library Acquisition Bond Issue
summary: Authorize up to $20 million in general obligation bonds for academic, public school, tribal, and public library resource acquisitions, funded by a statewide property tax
econReasoning: This measure funds public libraries through general obligation bonds backed by statewide property taxation, representing a left-aligned position favoring public sector investment in education and cultural resources. The funding mechanism relies on progressive taxation and public spending rather than private alternatives or tax reduction.
socialReasoning: Library acquisition has no direct bearing on social policy issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun control, criminal justice, or other culture war dimensions that define the social axis.

title: Repeal Separate Election Requirement for School Election Dates Amendment
summary: Repeal the constitutional provision requiring that school elections be held at different times from partisan elections
econReasoning: This measure addresses the procedural timing of school elections relative to partisan elections and has no direct impact on taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms.
socialReasoning: The measure concerns election administration logistics rather than social issues such as rights, freedoms, or cultural values.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a technical election timing reform, but repealing the separate election requirement could materially affect school board election dynamics by increasing voter overlap with partisan elections, potentially shifting power between school governance and partisan political mobilization.

title: Senior Citizen Facilities Bond Issue
summary: Authorize $30 million in general obligation bonds for senior citizen facility improvement, construction, and equipment acquisition projects, funded by a statewide property tax
econReasoning: This measure funds public social services through general obligation bonds repaid via progressive property taxation, which aligns with left/Democratic approaches to government spending and public provision. Bond-funded infrastructure for social welfare programs is characteristic of progressive fiscal policy.
socialReasoning: The measure addresses senior citizen facilities with no explicit social policy dimensions regarding abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting, drugs, religion, or traditional values. Senior services provision is not inherently partisan on the social axis.

title: Require Voter Identification Amendment
summary: Require photographic identification to vote for all voters, not just those voting in person
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it addresses voting procedures rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Voter ID requirements are a signature conservative/Republican position, aligned with concerns about election integrity and fraud prevention. This is a core social conservative policy that Republican-aligned groups consistently champion and Democratic-aligned groups typically oppose.

title: Constitutional Measure 1
summary: Create a single-subject requirement for initiated constitutional amendments and legislatively referred constitutional amendments in North Dakota
econReasoning: This measure addresses procedural rules for constitutional amendments and contains no provisions affecting taxation, spending, redistribution, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: This measure establishes a single-subject requirement for ballot initiatives and does not address social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or voting access.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a neutral procedural constraint on constitutional amendments, but imposing single-subject requirements on initiated amendments structurally reduces the power of direct democracy and ballot initiatives—a tool historically used more by progressive movements—relative to legislative authority.

title: Constitutional Measure 1
summary: Establish term limits for state legislators of four complete four-year terms in the state legislature regardless of chamber; provide that a term that is less than four-years does not count toward the term limit; repeal the constitutional provision established by Constitutional Measure 1 of 2022 which provided that term limits could only be amended or repealed by initiative petition and not by the state legislature
econReasoning: This measure concerns legislative term limits and constitutional amendment procedures, which do not directly address taxation, spending, regulation, or market-driven economic policy. The economic dimension is neutral.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses internal legislative procedures and term limits rather than social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting access, or gun rights. The social dimension is neutral.
framingNote: The measure is framed as establishing term limits but its structural effect includes repealing a 2022 constitutional protection that required initiative petitions (direct democracy) to amend or repeal term limits, shifting amendment power from the electorate to the legislature.

title: Constitutional Measure 2
summary: Require constitutional amendments to receive a 60% supermajority vote to be adopted
econReasoning: This measure establishes a procedural requirement for constitutional amendments and does not directly address taxation, spending, regulation, or economic redistribution. It is neutral on the economic axis.
socialReasoning: This measure is a structural change to the amendment process and does not address social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or other traditional left-right social policy divides. It is neutral on the social axis.
framingNote: While framed as a procedural amendment requirement, this measure structurally raises the barrier to constitutional change, which disproportionately benefits those seeking to preserve existing constitutional arrangements—a conservative institutional effect that may not be immediately apparent to voters viewing it as merely a technical voting threshold.

title: State Question 832
summary: Increase the minimum wage to $15.00 per hour
econReasoning: Minimum wage increases are a core progressive/left economic policy that redistributes income upward to lower-wage workers and increases labor regulation on employers. This aligns with Democratic and labor-oriented positions that prioritize worker protections over market-driven wage determination.
socialReasoning: Minimum wage policy has no inherent connection to social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting rights, or other cultural divides that define the social axis.

title: State Question 844
summary: Require the state legislature to establish a methodology for reimbursing schools, local governments, service districts, and other local organizations for revenues lost due to a manufacturing tax exemption
econReasoning: This measure requires the state to reimburse local entities for revenue losses from manufacturing tax exemptions, effectively limiting the fiscal benefit of those exemptions and shifting costs toward the state budget. While manufacturing tax exemptions themselves are business-friendly (conservative), this measure constrains their implementation by imposing a compensatory funding requirement, reflecting a more cautious fiscal approach aligned with protecting public revenues.

title: State Question 846
summary: Require voters to provide proof of identity when voting in any election in the state
econReasoning: Voter ID requirements have no direct economic dimension—they do not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: Voter ID requirements are a signature conservative/Republican priority, typically supported by the right as an election security measure and opposed by the left as a voting rights restriction. This measure aligns with the conservative social agenda on voting procedure.

title: State Question 845
summary: Make changes to the judicial nominating commission, including that appointments shall be made according to current congressional districts
econReasoning: This measure concerns judicial appointment procedures and does not directly address taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms, placing it outside the economic axis.
socialReasoning: The measure shifts judicial nominating commission appointments to congressional district-based selection, a procedural change that Republicans typically favor as it can increase political influence over judicial selection and reduce centralized appointing authority. This aligns with conservative preferences for greater electoral/political influence over courts.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a technical change to appointment procedures, but structurally shifts power over judicial selection from a nominating commission toward partisan congressional district representation, which increases direct political control over the judiciary.

title: Referendum 120
summary: Repeal sections of House Bill 3991 that increase the state's gas tax, payroll tax, and vehicle registration fees
econReasoning: This referendum seeks to repeal tax increases (gas tax, payroll tax, vehicle registration fees), which aligns with right-leaning fiscal policy favoring lower taxes and reduced government revenue. The measure directly opposes progressive revenue-raising mechanisms established in HB 3991.
socialReasoning: The measure has no significant social dimension; it addresses only fiscal and tax policy without touching social issues like abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, or other culturally divisive topics.

title: Constitutional Amendment I
summary: Condition Medicaid expansion on federal funding remaining at least 90%
econReasoning: This measure constrains Medicaid expansion by conditioning it on federal funding remaining at or above 90%, effectively limiting the state's ability to expand public healthcare coverage. This aligns with Republican/right-leaning fiscal conservatism that resists public program expansion and prioritizes fiscal constraint over healthcare access.
socialReasoning: The measure has no direct social dimension related to the listed social policy areas (abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting, drugs, religion, immigration, etc.).

title: Constitutional Amendment J
summary: Provide that "[an] individual who is not a citizen of the United States is disqualified from voting in any election or upon any question submitted to the voters"
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it addresses voting eligibility rather than taxation, spending, regulation, or market policy.
socialReasoning: This amendment restricts voting rights based on citizenship status, a core conservative immigration-restriction position aligned with Republican priorities. Citizenship-based voting eligibility requirements are standard right-aligned social policy positions.

title: Constitutional Amendment K
summary: Establish an unclaimed property trust fund
econReasoning: Establishing an unclaimed property trust fund is a modest fiscal management measure that involves state stewardship of dormant assets rather than redistribution or tax policy. It has a slightly conservative orientation toward property rights and fund management but lacks significant ideological economic content.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses fiscal administration of unclaimed property and contains no provisions relating to social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, criminal justice, voting, drugs, religion, or gun rights.

title: Constitutional Amendment L
summary: Require a 60% majority by voters to approve constitutional amendments
econReasoning: This measure concerns the procedural threshold for approving constitutional amendments and has no direct economic dimension regarding taxation, spending, regulation, or market policy.
socialReasoning: This measure is procedural rather than substantive and does not directly address social issues such as rights, freedoms, or cultural values; it neither restricts nor expands any particular social policy.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a neutral 'supermajority requirement' for constitutional amendments, but it structurally shifts power by raising the voter threshold needed to amend the state constitution, making it harder for future ballot initiatives to succeed regardless of their content—an effect that benefits those seeking to block constitutional change.

title: Amendment 1
summary: Remove the right to bail for specified criminal offenses
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension; it does not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: Removing bail rights for specified offenses aligns with conservative criminal justice positions favoring stricter detention policies and reduced judicial discretion. This reflects a rightward stance on law-and-order criminal justice reform, which is predominantly a Republican/conservative priority.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a constitutional amendment to 'remove the right to bail' but structurally functions as a mandatory detention provision that eliminates judicial discretion over bail decisions for certain offenses, masking the removal of judicial flexibility behind constitutional language.

title: Amendment 2
summary: Prohibit the legislature from enacting taxes on property
econReasoning: This measure prohibits property taxation by the state legislature, a core tax-cut and anti-government-revenue position strongly aligned with Republican/right fiscal ideology. Removing a major revenue source constrains government spending and reflects market-oriented tax policy preferences.
socialReasoning: The measure addresses taxation and revenue mechanisms with no direct connection to social policy dimensions such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or other cultural issues.

title: Amendment 3
summary: Amend the list of crime victims' rights in the constitution
econReasoning: This measure addresses constitutional rights enumeration for crime victims and has no direct economic dimension regarding taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Expanding crime victims' rights in the constitution aligns with conservative criminal justice priorities that emphasize victim protections and support for law enforcement, positions typically associated with the political right.
framingNote: The measure uses 'victims' rights' language to frame what is structurally an expansion of procedural and substantive protections that shift the balance toward prosecution and victim involvement in criminal proceedings, rather than a neutral enumeration of pre-existing rights.

title: 60% Vote Requirement for Ballot Initiatives to Increase or Expand Taxes Amendment
summary: Require a supermajority requirement of 60% by voters for citizen-initiated ballot measures that create new taxes, increase existing tax rates, or for a property tax
econReasoning: This measure makes it harder for voters to approve tax increases through ballot initiatives by imposing a 60% supermajority requirement, aligning with conservative fiscal policy goals of constraining tax growth and limiting the expansion of public revenue. The measure structurally favors tax restraint and reduced government spending capacity.
socialReasoning: This measure concerns fiscal procedure and tax policy only, with no substantive connection to social issues such as abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, or other culture-war domains.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a tax-restraint mechanism but structurally shifts power from the electorate (simple majority rule) to a supermajority threshold, effectively privileging the status quo and making voter-initiated tax policy changes substantially harder to enact.

title: Publication Requirements for Constitutional Amendments Measure
summary: Require that constitutional amendments appearing on the ballot must be published for a period of 60 days
econReasoning: This measure establishes a procedural requirement for constitutional amendment publication and does not directly address taxation, spending, redistribution, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: This measure concerns the procedural mechanics of how constitutional amendments reach voters and contains no substantive policy position on social issues such as rights, freedoms, or moral questions.

title: Proposal 3
summary: Establish a state constitutional right to organize or join a labor organization for the purpose of collective bargaining
econReasoning: Labor organizing and collective bargaining rights are core left-aligned economic positions that redistribute power from employers to workers and typically increase labor costs and regulation. This constitutional protection would strengthen union organizing capacity, a signature Democratic/progressive economic priority.
socialReasoning: This measure addresses labor rights and does not meaningfully engage with the social axis dimensions (abortion, LGBTQ+ protections, gun rights, criminal justice, etc.), making it neutral on the social spectrum.

title: Use of Legislative Congressional Redistricting Map Amendment
summary: Allow the state legislature to conduct congressional redistricting between 2025 and 2030
econReasoning: This measure concerns the procedural mechanism for redistricting and has no direct economic policy content regarding taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Congressional redistricting conducted by a legislature (rather than an independent commission) typically benefits the party in power to draw favorable district boundaries. Republicans currently control Virginia's legislature and would predominantly support this measure to enable partisan gerrymandering, making it align with conservative political interests.
framingNote: The measure is framed as simply allowing the legislature to conduct redistricting, but structurally it shifts power from an independent redistricting commission back to the legislature, enabling partisan gerrymandering that affects voting representation and electoral outcomes.

title: Remove Constitutional Same-Sex Marriage Ban Amendment
summary: Repeal the provision defining marriage as between one man and one woman and prohibit the state from denying a marriage license to two adult persons based on sex, gender, or race
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation of commerce, or distribution of resources.
socialReasoning: Repealing a same-sex marriage ban and explicitly protecting marriage rights regardless of sex, gender, or race is a core progressive/left position aligned with LGBTQ+ protections and rejection of traditional family restrictions that the right typically defends.

title: Right to Reproductive Freedom Amendment
summary: Provide a state constitutional right to reproductive freedom, defined as "the right to make and effectuate one's own decisions about all matters related to one's pregnancy"
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: This measure establishes a constitutional right to abortion and pregnancy-related decisions, which is a core progressive/Democratic-aligned position on the social axis. It directly protects reproductive autonomy and opposes abortion restrictions, aligning strongly with left-wing social values.

title: Voting Rights Restoration for Felons Upon Release Amendment
summary: Provide that persons convicted of felonies have their voting rights restored upon release from incarceration
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it does not involve taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or resource distribution.
socialReasoning: Voting rights restoration for individuals convicted of felonies is a criminal justice reform measure that aligns with progressive/left positions on criminal justice, voting rights expansion, and rehabilitation over permanent disenfranchisement—positions consistently championed by Democratic and progressive constituencies.

title: Parental Right to Review Education Materials, Receive Notifications, and Opt Out of Sexual-Health Education Initiative
summary: Re-enact Initiative to the Legislature 2081 (2024), related to parental rights to review educational materials, receive specific notifications, and opt their children out of sexual health education
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it does not address taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or fiscal policy.
socialReasoning: This measure strongly aligns with conservative social positions by expanding parental authority over education content, particularly sexual health education, and establishing notification and opt-out rights—positions typically championed by Republicans and social conservatives who emphasize traditional family authority over curricula.
framingNote: The measure frames parental oversight as a 'right' when structurally it functions as a restriction on comprehensive sex education curriculum and information access, limiting what schools can teach without parental intervention.

title: Sex Verification Requirements for Female School Sports Initiative
summary: Require school and voluntary nonprofit athletics to prohibit biologically male students from competing with and against biologically female students
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension; it addresses athletic eligibility rules rather than taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or resource distribution.
socialReasoning: This measure restricts transgender and sex-diverse students' participation in school sports based on biological sex categorization, aligning with conservative social positions on traditional gender definitions and anti-trans policies that are predominantly backed by Republican voters.
framingNote: The measure uses 'sex verification' and 'biologically male' framing to characterize a restriction on student athletic participation, which presents a exclusionary policy in ostensibly objective biological terms rather than acknowledging it as a policy choice about trans student inclusion.

title: Citizenship Voting Requirement Amendment
summary: Amend the constitution to state that only citizens may vote in elections
econReasoning: This measure has no economic dimension; it does not address taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms.
socialReasoning: Citizenship voting requirements are a core conservative/right position on immigration and voting access. Republicans have consistently advocated for citizenship verification in voting, while Democrats have generally opposed such restrictions as barriers to voting rights. This aligns strongly with the right side of the social axis.

title: Prohibit Government Closure of Places of Worship During Emergencies Amendment
summary: Prohibit the government from closing gatherings at places of worship during declared emergencies, including public health emergencies
econReasoning: This measure has no significant economic dimension—it does not address taxation, spending, regulation of markets, or redistribution of resources.
socialReasoning: This amendment protects religious gatherings from government closure during emergencies, functioning as a religious exemption that prioritizes religious exercise over public health authority—a position strongly aligned with conservative social priorities.
framingNote: The measure frames a restriction on government emergency powers as protecting 'rights,' but structurally establishes a religious exemption from otherwise-applicable emergency health orders, which is a conservative policy position rather than a neutral liberty expansion.

title: Prohibit Government Discrimination or Preferential Treatment Amendment
summary: Prohibit the government from discriminating or granting preferential treatment based on race, sex, color, ethnicity, or national origin
econReasoning: This measure does not address taxation, spending, regulation, or market mechanisms, so it has no meaningful economic dimension.
socialReasoning: This amendment prohibits race- and sex-based government discrimination and preferential treatment, positions strongly aligned with conservative and Republican opposition to affirmative action and diversity programs. Republican voters and conservative organizations have championed such measures, while Democratic voters and progressive organizations typically oppose them.
framingNote: The measure uses 'prohibit discrimination' language that obscures its structural purpose: restricting race- and sex-conscious government policies (including affirmative action and diversity initiatives) rather than expanding protected categories, which progressives would frame as restricting civil rights remedies.

title: Prohibit Partial Veto to Increase Tax or Fee Amendment
summary: Prohibit the governor from using the partial veto to create or increase any tax or fee
econReasoning: This measure restricts gubernatorial power to increase taxes or fees through partial veto, aligning with conservative fiscal restraint and tax limitation principles. It constrains executive authority over revenue-raising mechanisms, a core right-leaning fiscal priority.
socialReasoning: This measure concerns procedural fiscal authority and contains no substantive social policy dimension related to cultural, civil rights, or criminal justice issues.
framingNote: The measure is framed as a tax/fee prohibition but structurally reallocates executive-legislative balance by constraining a specific gubernatorial power, which may not be immediately apparent to voters focused on the tax-limiting language.

title: Homeowner's Primary Residence Property Tax Exemption Initiative
summary: Create a homeowner's property tax exemption that exempts 50% of a primary residence's assessed value from property taxation
econReasoning: This measure reduces property tax revenue by exempting 50% of primary residence assessed value, consistent with Republican-aligned tax-cut and reduced-government-revenue positions. The exemption benefits homeowners directly through lower taxation, aligning with conservative fiscal priorities.
socialReasoning: This is a purely fiscal measure concerning property taxation and has no discernible social dimension related to the social axis issues (abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, voting rights, drug policy, or religious exemptions).

title: Marsy's Law Crime Victims Rights Amendment
summary: Amend the list of crime victims' rights in the constitution
econReasoning: Marsy's Law has no significant economic dimension; it concerns procedural rights for crime victims rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Crime victims' rights amendments align with conservative criminal justice priorities by strengthening protections for crime victims in legal proceedings, a position traditionally supported by Republicans and law-and-order advocates.
framingNote: The measure uses 'victims' rights' language to frame constitutional amendments that substantively expand state power and victim participation in criminal proceedings, which could materially constrain defendant protections and judicial discretion in ways not immediately apparent from the 'rights' framing.

title: Prohibit State Property Taxes Amendment
summary: Prohibit the legislature from enacting taxes on property
econReasoning: This amendment prohibits state property taxes, a core conservative tax-cutting position that aligns with right-wing fiscal policy favoring reduced government revenue and deregulation. Property tax elimination is a hallmark Republican/libertarian economic priority.
socialReasoning: This measure has no direct social dimension; it does not address abortion, LGBTQ+ rights, gun rights, criminal justice, voting access, drug policy, or other socially salient issues.

title: Remove Right to Bail for Certain Criminal Offenses Amendment
summary: Remove the right to bail for specified criminal offenses
econReasoning: This measure has no direct economic dimension, as it addresses procedural criminal justice rules rather than taxation, spending, or market regulation.
socialReasoning: Removing the right to bail for certain offenses aligns with conservative criminal justice positions that prioritize public safety and judicial discretion to detain dangerous defendants, opposing progressive bail reform efforts that emphasize pretrial release rights.
framingNote: The measure's title frames it neutrally as 'removal of right to bail for certain offenses,' but structurally it eliminates judicial discretion over bail decisions for specified crimes, concentrating power in the hands of prosecutors and reducing individual due process protections—a significant procedural restriction obscured by the straightforward framing.
